revision.text:
[[ceb:Imba-numa]][[en:Lake Inba]][[fr:Lac Inba]][[ja:印旛沼]][[ko:인바늪]][[ru:Инбанума]] {{Infobox Lake |name=Lake Inba (印旛沼) |image=[[File:Lake Inba.JPG|280px|View from Funado Bridge (Inzai City)]] |location={{JPN}}
{{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}} ([[Inzai City]], [[Sakura City]], [[Narita City]], [[Yachiyo City]], [[Sakae Town]])
{{日本の位置情報|35|48|0|140|15|0|Lake Inba|35.8,140.25|Lake Inba}}
{{Location map|Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan Kanto#Japan |width=200|float=center|relief=1|caption=Location of Lake Inba}} |area=9.43{{Cite web|和書|author=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|authorlink=国土地理院|date=2015-03-06|title=平成26th year 全国都道府県市区町村別面積調 湖沼面積|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/KOKUJYOHO/MENCHO/backnumber/GSI-menseki20141001.pdf|format=PDF|accessdate=2015-03-22}} |shore_length=47.5 |max_depth=2.5 |average_depth=1.7 |volume=0.0277 |elevation=2.5 (Y.P.) during irrigation period |origin=River-dammed lake |fresh_salinity=Freshwater |lake_type=Eutrophic lake |transparency=0.7 }} {{Maplink2|zoom=10|frame=yes|plain=no|frame-align=right|frame-width=250|frame-height=250 |type=shape|fill=#87cefa|stroke-color=#0000ff|stroke-width=1|id=Q63860206|title='''North Lake Inba''' |type2=shape|fill2=#87cefa|stroke-color2=#0000ff|stroke-width2=1|id2=Q63860210|title2='''West Lake Inba''' }} '''Lake Inba''' (印旛沼, Inbanuma) is a lake in the Tone River system located in northern {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, Japan. It is part of the [[Chiba Prefectural Inba-Tega Natural Park]]. Designated under the [[Law for the Protection of Lake Water Quality]], it is also recognized as one of the [[Top 100 Japanese Reservoirs]]. == Geography == Located on the right bank of the lower Tone River, in the center of the {{ill|Shimōsa Plateau|en|Shimōsa Plateau|qid=Q5120650|lt=Shimōsa Plateau|1=Shimōsa Plateau}}, Lake Inba spans the municipalities of [[Inzai City]], [[Sakura City]], [[Narita City]], [[Yachiyo City]], and [[Sakae Town]]. Originally, it was a larger "W"-shaped lake with an area of 25.8 square kilometers, a depth of 1.8 meters, and a circumference of 60 kilometers.{{Cite web|和書|title=印旛沼(いんばぬま)とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8D%B0%E6%97%9B%E6%B2%BC-33212|website=Kotobank|accessdate=2019-03-20}} Post-war reclamation divided it into two water bodies, connected by narrow channels: the '''Northern Adjustment Reservoir''' ('''North Lake Inba''') and the '''Western Adjustment Reservoir''' ('''West Lake Inba'''). Despite its reduced size, it remains the largest lake in Chiba Prefecture. The southern shore of the lake is served by the {{ill|Keisei Main Line|en|Keisei Main Line|qid=Q1063675|lt=Keisei Main Line|1=Keisei Main Line}}, with Sakura City’s urban area nearby. To the east lies [[Narita New Town]], and to the northwest is {{ill|Chiba New Town|en|Chiba New Town|qid=Q11405878|lt=Chiba New Town|1=Chiba New Town}}. The [[:en:Narita Sky Access|Narita Sky Access]] and [[North Chiba Road]] cross North Lake Inba. The watershed covers an area of 487.18 square kilometers, with a population density of 727,000. This makes it the third most populous watershed in Japan, following the {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|qid=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} and {{ill|Lake Kasumigaura|en|Lake Kasumigaura|qid=Q1077984|lt=Lake Kasumigaura|1=Lake Kasumigaura}} basins. Normally, water from Lake Inba flows into the Tone River via the [[Nagato River]]. However, during internal flooding or backflow from the Tone River, the water drains from West Lake Inba into {{ill|Tokyo Bay|en|Tokyo Bay|qid=Q141017|lt=Tokyo Bay|1=Tokyo Bay}} via the [[Inba Drainage Canal]] (Shin-Kawa and Hanami River). === Inflow and Outflow Rivers === [[File:Inbanuma landsat.jpg|right|250px|thumb|Satellite view]] {{File clip|ChibakenYouranMap 1925.jpg|width=250|29|48|58|38|w=721|h=768|1925 map of Lake Inba (Chiba Prefecture Almanac)}} [[File:Inbanuma aerial shoot.jpg|thumb|250px|Aerial view of North Lake Inba (2023)]] All rivers belong to the Tone River system: * '''North Lake Inba''' ''' Ega River ''' [[Monoki Drainage]] ''' Matsumushi River ''' [[Nagato River]] (outflow) ''' Old Nagato River (outflow) * Inba Shortcut (Central Drainage Canal) ''' Nakagawa River * '''West Lake Inba''' ''' [[Inba Drainage Canal]] (Shin-Kawa) ''' Iwato Drainage ''' [[Taguri River]] ''' [[Moroto River]] ''' [[Kashima River (Chiba)|Kashima River]] (largest inflow) File:Kita inbanuma.JPG|'''North Lake Inba''' (elevated rail in foreground is Narita Sky Access) File:Nishi inbnuma.JPG|'''West Lake Inba''' (view from Inba Sunset Hills) == History == Lake Inba originated approximately 20,000 years ago as a valley eroded into the Shimōsa Plateau. During the [[Jomon transgression]], it became a submerged valley, part of the {{ill|Katori Sea|en|Katori Sea|qid=Q11667182|lt=Katori Sea|1=Katori Sea}} (ancient Kinu Bay). By the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, sediment carried by floods from the [[:en:Kinu River|Kinu River]] formed deltas, blocking the sea outlet and forming a lake. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the [[Tone River Diversion Project]] redirected the Tone River to protect {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} from flooding, increasing the frequency of flooding in the surrounding villages. This led to land reclamation projects for flood control and food supply. Major reclamation efforts during the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|qid=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō|1=Kyōhō}} era (1724) and {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|qid=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei|1=Tenmei}} era (1781–1789) were unsuccessful. Later, the {{ill|Tenpō Reforms|en|Tenpō Reforms|qid=Q2367979|lt=Tenpō Reforms|1=Tenpō Reforms}} (1843) and other projects also failed to complete the planned waterways. The 20th century saw renewed efforts for drainage and flood control. The [[Inba Drainage Canal]] was completed in the late 1960s, and the central reclamation area was created in 1969, reducing the lake’s area by half. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of lakes in Japan#Kanto|ja|日本の湖沼一覧#関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of lakes in Japan#Kanto|1=List of lakes of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1540607}} * {{ill|Lake Tega|en|Lake Tega|qid=Q3215223|lt=Teganuma|1=Lake Tega}} * {{ill|Tone River Diversion Project|ja|利根川東遷事業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tone River Diversion Project|1=Tone River Eastward Diversion Project|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11396898}} * {{ill|Inba Reclamation|ja|印旛沼干拓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inba Reclamation|1=Reclamation of Lake Inban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17193819}} * {{ill|Tanuma Okitsugu|en|Tanuma Okitsugu|qid=Q521070|lt=Tanuma Okitsugu|1=Tanuma Okitsugu}} * {{ill|Inzai|en|Inzai|qid=Q637901|lt=Inzai City|1=Inzai}} (formerly [[Inba Village]] and [[Motonō Village]]) * {{ill|Lake Inba Dragon Myth|ja|印旛沼の竜伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lake Inba Dragon Myth|1=Dragon Legend of Lake Inban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11409094}} ''' {{ill|Ryūkaku-ji|en|Ryūkaku-ji|qid=Q11679369|lt=Ryūkakuji|1=Ryūkaku-ji}}, {{ill|Ryūfukuji|ja|龍腹寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūfukuji|1=Ryūfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11679347}}, and {{ill|Ryūbi-ji|ja|龍尾寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūbi-ji|1=Ryūbi-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11679230}} * {{ill|Lake Inba Monster|ja|印旛沼の怪獣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lake Inba Monster|1=The Lake Inba Monster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q107410823}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Lake Inba}} * [https://inba-numa.com Lake Inba Information Plaza] * [https://www.water.go.jp/kanto/chiba/index.html Water Resources Agency: Lake Inba Development] * [https://www.google.co.jp/maps?q=%E5%8D%B0%E6%97%9B%E6%B2%BC&hl=ja&ll=35.800133,140.25301&spn=0.075879,0.116386&z=13&iwloc=addr&om=1 Google Maps: Lake Inba] {{日本の湖沼}}{{千葉県立公園}}
